questions.prompt:
(25 points) Various organizations rank universities worldwide every year. These rankings
are often controversial and do not always agree with each other. Alice wants to find out
the level of (in)consistency between the QS ranking and the US News ranking. Consider n
universities. For each university 1 <i <n, qj and u; are its ranks accoording to QS and US
News respectively. (Hence, qi, q2,-..,;@n and uj, U2,...Un are two permutations of 1 to n.)
We call a pair of universities 7,7 a “disagreement” if q > q; and u; < u;. Design a divide
and conquer algorithm for finding the number of “disagreements” between the QS and US
News rankings. Your algorithm shall have running time strictly faster than O(n?); to get
full credit, your algorithm shall have running time O(nlogn).

questions.prompt:
(25 points) The computer science department was recently gifted with n coupons for Ama-
zon Web Services (AWS), each of which is worth 100k USD. There are m > n faculty
members in the department, each belongs to a research group. We will denote the faculty
members as 1 to m, and the research groups as G1, G2,...,G, which form a partition of 1
to m. Bob, the department head, wants to find a fair and efficient way to distribute these
coupons. Each faculty member has written a proposal for how he/she would use the coupon
to enhance his/her research. Based on the proposals, Bob has come up with a value v; > 0
for giving a coupon to each faculty member 1 <7i< m. (You may assume that v,’s are
distinct.) Further, Bob decides that each researh group gets at most c coupons for some
cap | <c<n. Design a greedy algorithm to distribute the coupons to n faculty members
subject to this cap, such that the sum of their values is maximized. To get full credit, you
need to also provide an argument for the algorithm’s correctness and running time.
questions.taggingError: No syllabus topics available during extraction.

questions.prompt:
(25 points) Professor Charlie is an economist who works at city A in Chile. He is invited
to deliver a keynote speech at city B in China on July 15, 2023. Since A and B are both
small cities and locate on the opposite sides of the globe, he needs to take multiple flights
to get to city B. Prof. Charlie is very busy and cannot leave city A before 8am on July
13 (for simplicity, the time in this question has been converted to Hong Kong time). He
wants to know if he could arrive at city B by 9pm on July 14. For that, he turns to you,
his computer science friend at Hong Kong. Suppose that you are given a list of all flights
worldwide, each represented by the take-off city and time, and landing city and time. (For
simplicity, assume that all flights will be on time.) A feasible schedule is a sequence of
flights so that:
1. The first flight takes off after 8am on July 13 (inclusive) from city A;
2. The landing city of each flight is the take-off city of the next flight;
3. The landing time of each flight is at least 1 hour (inclusive) earlier than the next flight;
4. The last flight lands at city B before 9pm on July 14 (inclusive).
(a) (15 points) Design a polynomial-time algorithm that either finds such a schedule and
reports the sequence of flights, or concludes that no such schedule exists.
(b) (10 points) Revise your algorithm such that it returns the schedule that arrives the
earliest when there are multiple feasible schedules.
To get full credit, you also need to provide an argument for the algorithm’s correctness and
running time.

questions.prompt:
(25 points) Consider a set of jobs Jj, J2,---,Jn. Each job J; has a required processing
time p; and a deadline d;, where p; and d; are positive integers. The jobs are sorted in a
non-decreasing order of deadlines (i.e., dy < dz <--- < dy). Scheduling a job J; = (pj, d;)
requires assigning an interval |t,t-+p;) exclusively to J;, where 0 < t < d;—p,;. Different jobs
must be scheduled in non-overlapping intervals. For example, J; = (4,5) and Jz = (5,10)
can be scheduled in [0,4) and [4, 9), respectively.
Given a set of jobs, it might not be feasible to schedule all the jobs (e.g., J; = (5,6) and
Jq = (5,8)); the objective is to schedule the maximum number of jobs.
(a) (5 points) Consider the following greedy algorithm. Denote ACCEPTED to be an
empty set initially. Starting from Jj, repeatedly pick the next job Jj, if scheduling
J; immediately after all the jobs already in ACCEPTED can meet the deadline of J;,
add J; into ACCEPTED and schedule it accordingly; otherwise, discard J;. Give an
example to show that this algorithm fails to schedule the maximum number of jobs.
(b) (20 points) Show how to use dynamic programming to compute the maximum number
of jobs that can be scheduled together. The time complexity should depend on the
number of jobs and the value of the maximum deadline.

questions.prompt:
(25 points) In the year of 2046, GPT-11 has evolved in to a super-AI that threatens the very
existence of humanity. The world government therefore decides to cut off its power supply.
In this simplified problem, we model the power network as an undirected graph G = (V, E),
whose vertices are substations and edges are powerlines. There are two specical vertices s
and t, where s represents the power plant and t represents the GPT-11 server. Shutting
down each substation v € V \{s,t} has a positive integer cost cy. Design a polynomial-time
algorithm for choosing a subset of substations S such that shutting them down disconnects
s and t, and the total cost is minimized.
